Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd have these bursts where I'd tell myself I was going to get organized, but then I'd just kind of drift through the week without much structure. I'd usually end up cramming when something was due, listening to lectures while doing other things around my flat. I have good resources at home, so I never felt urgency to be more disciplined about it. Looking back, 14 hours a week sounds like a lot but it was spread thin and unfocused.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and whatever else was going on in my life. I'm 24 and involved in extracurriculars, so there was always something competing for my attention. Some weeks I'd barely touch coursework, other weeks I'd panic and try to catch up. I never really established those regular study times that probably would have helped me stay on track.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or those gamified task notifications that got me to log in. I actually found the game elements kind of fun at first—they gave me little dopamine hits that made logging in feel less like a chore. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was something new or interactive to do. But I'll admit, sometimes I'd log in, poke around, and then get distracted by something else.
Q4: For a brief period, the gamified elements made logging in feel almost habitual. I liked earning points and seeing progress bars fill up—it appealed to something in me. But that wore off after a while when I realized the actual coursework underneath was still hard and I wasn't really absorbing it. The routine never fully stuck because my motivation was tied to the game mechanics, not the learning itself.
Q5: The audio lectures and recorded discussions were genuinely useful for me since I learn best by listening. I could put them on while commuting or doing chores, which fit my lifestyle. The gamified tasks also kept me coming back more than dry readings would have. When something felt interactive or had an audio component, I was way more likely to actually engage with it.
Q6: The dense reading materials and long PDFs—I basically ignored those. I'd download them with good intentions but they'd just sit there. Without internet at home, I couldn't always access things when I wanted, so I'd batch download stuff and then never get to it. The discussion forums also felt like a lot of effort for unclear payoff, so I only posted when I felt like I had something quick to say.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was either genuinely interesting or clearly connected to something I needed to submit. The gamified tasks hit that sweet spot because they felt like accomplishments even if they were small. If something was vague about its purpose or felt like busywork, I'd put it off indefinitely. I needed to see the point immediately or I'd lose interest.
Q8: Optional activities felt like nice-to-haves that I'd get to 'eventually'—which usually meant never. Required ones at least had the pressure of consequences, so I'd force myself through them even when I didn't want to. The optional stuff felt lower stakes, which sounds freeing but actually made it easier to ignore. I wish I'd done more of them, but in the moment, required work always won.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lectures and listen to them on my phone since I don't have internet at home. I'd also talk through concepts with friends from my extracurriculars who were taking similar courses. A lot of my studying happened through conversations and listening to recordings while doing other things. The LMS wouldn't capture any of that audio-based, offline learning.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was genuinely thinking about course concepts while walking around or during downtime, but obviously that doesn't show up anywhere. I'd listen to a downloaded lecture twice while cooking and cleaning, working through ideas in my head. My peer interactions happened a lot in person, not on the platform. The LMS only saw me when I was actively clicking, not when I was actually processing.
Q11: All the time, yeah. I'd access a lecture once to download it, then listen to it multiple times offline over the next few days. Same with any audio content—one download, repeated listens. The LMS probably thought I only engaged with it briefly when really I was getting way more out of it than that single access suggested. It's just how I had to work around not having internet at home.
Q12: There were definitely periods where everything felt overwhelming and I just checked out. My stress levels got pretty intense mid-semester, and with moderate anxiety on top of that, I'd freeze up instead of tackling things. When multiple deadlines piled up or I fell behind, it became easier to avoid the course entirely than face how much I'd let slip. My resilience isn't great, so setbacks hit me hard.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or stressed from extracurriculars, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Confusion about what was actually required versus optional made me procrastinate because I didn't know where to start. Competing responsibilities always seemed more immediate than coursework that wasn't due yet. By the time deadlines arrived, I was often too fatigued to do quality work.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd log in, click through some materials, maybe watch part of a video, but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd be going through the motions to feel productive without actually learning anything. Sometimes I'd have the gamified tasks open but be half-focused on my phone. The activity looked like engagement but it was pretty hollow.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they mattered. Part of it was anxiety—the bigger something felt, the more I'd avoid starting it. Part of it was just that other things seemed more appealing in the moment. I'd tell myself I worked better under pressure, but really I was just making excuses. By the time I finally started, I didn't have enough time to do well.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that reliably got me to actually submit work. My LMS usage would spike right before things were due—classic cramming pattern. Without a deadline, I'd drift and tell myself I'd get to it later. The structure of having due dates was necessary for me, even though I resented feeling controlled by them. I wish I'd spread things out more.
Q17: Feedback was a mixed bag for me. When it was encouraging, it gave me a brief boost, but when it pointed out problems, I'd feel discouraged and sometimes avoid engaging for a while. I didn't always check feedback promptly because I was anxious about what it might say. The gamified elements gave more immediate positive feedback, which I responded to better than written comments on assignments.
Q18: The structure felt both helpful and overwhelming. Having twelve online courses meant there was always something I should be doing, which created constant low-level guilt. The gamified task intervention actually helped give me entry points that felt manageable. But the overall volume of content made it easy to fall behind and hard to catch up once I did.
Q19: The gamified elements definitely made engagement easier—they gave me small wins that kept me coming back. What made it harder was the sheer amount of reading-heavy content that didn't suit my auditory learning style. Also, without reliable internet, I couldn't always access things when motivation struck. The course wasn't really designed for someone who learns best by listening.
Q20: I think that was me, honestly. I logged in a lot, did the gamified tasks, participated in discussions here and there, but my actual assignment completion and exam performance didn't match that activity. Sometimes engagement is about showing up without really producing. You can be present in the system without doing the deep work that leads to good submissions.
Q21: That makes sense to me too. If someone's a good test-taker or learns efficiently outside the platform, they might not need to be constantly logged in. I wasn't that person—my offline studying didn't translate to exam success for me. But I can see how someone with better study strategies or more resilience could do well without high LMS activity.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how often you show up. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the learning that happens offline. They can't tell if you're actually understanding something or just going through motions. For someone like me who learns by listening and discusses things in person, the log misses a huge chunk of my actual engagement.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort that leads somewhere. It's not just showing up—it's being mentally present and doing work that builds understanding. I was engaged in some superficial ways this semester, but not in the deep way that would have helped me pass. Real engagement should feel purposeful, not just like checking boxes.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally, or how my anxiety affected my ability to focus even when I was logged in. It wouldn't capture the conversations with friends that helped me understand concepts, or the lectures I replayed while offline. It also wouldn't show that despite failing, I actually found some of the content interesting—I just couldn't translate that interest into results.
